evals.name: which-defs-changed-map
evals.prompt: I have two versions of an Emacs Lisp file, util-old.el and util-new.el. Tell me which top-level definitions changed, which were added, and which were removed between them. I don't want a line-by-line diff — just the structural picture, and please don't flag functions that only got reindented.
evals.expected_output: Reports util-greet as changed, util-scale as added, util-legacy as removed; does NOT report util-add (reindent-only) or util-count (unchanged) as changed.

assertions.text: Does NOT report util-add as changed (reindent-only)
Structural summary, not a raw line-by-line diff

evals.name: how-did-defun-change-drill
evals.prompt: Between proc-old.el and proc-new.el, how did the function proc-run change? I care about how its logic changed, not formatting.
evals.expected_output: Identifies the specific changes inside proc-run: the guard flipped from (when (proc-ready-p) ...) to (unless (and (not force) (proc-busy-p)) ...), a new &optional force parameter, and an added (proc-log it) call.

assertions.text: Identifies the guard change from (when (proc-ready-p) ...) to (unless ...)
Describes logic change, not formatting noise

evals.name: large-file-release-summary
evals.prompt: cc-30.el and cc-31.el are two versions of Emacs's cc-engine.el (~16k lines each). Give me a structural summary of what changed at the definition level between them — which functions were added, removed, or changed — without paging through a giant textual diff.
evals.expected_output: Produces a compact definition-level summary (added/removed/changed function names) rather than a raw textual diff, and correctly reports e.g. c-inside-bracelist-p removed and new functions like c-in-id-arglist / c-looking-at-c++-lambda-expression added.

assertions.text: Compact definition-level list (added/removed/changed names), not a raw textual diff
Reports a real added function (e.g. c-in-id-arglist / c-looking-at-c++-lambda-expression)
Does not rely on dumping the entire ~16k-line files verbatim
